sections.directions: Directions: Each set of questions in this section is based on a single passage. The questions are to be answered on the basis of what is stated or implied in the passage. For some questions, more than one of the choices could conceivably answer the question. However, you are to choose the best answer; that is, choose the response that most accurately and completely answers the question.

items.stimulusText:
While best known for her fiction, Edith Wharton (1862–1937) also wrote extensively on architecture and decor. Her focus was on the "designer" who must work within the confines of an existing house, producing a good design upon the background of a faulty and illogical structure. This led to an extended meditation on the aesthetics of enclosed spaces, in which the very limitations that constrain artistic expression are seen as providing the framework that makes it possible. This theme of enclosure appears in her fiction as well, serving there as a metaphor for the multifarious constraints imposed by society and tradition.

Around the turn of the century these constraints were nowhere more evident than in the position of women in society. Although her fiction dealt compassionately with the intricacies and tragedies of middle-class women's lives, Wharton, unlike many feminists of her time, was pessimistic about the possibility of shattering the traditional barriers to women's advancement. Instead she pursued a different but still feminist path, in the belief that women's best hope lay in gaining economic self-sufficiency within the confines of traditional society. To this end, she made herself an astute, self-reliant manager of her own career. She supported herself by writing at a time when most women writers were either unable to do so or were forced, unlike Wharton, to write under male pseudonyms. Her skepticism about the value of more radical social change reflected a basic faith in many aspects of traditional society. The metaphor of enclosure expressed her ambivalence toward a society that she perceived as both confining and at the same time providing needed structure.

This ambivalence was expressed in Wharton's aesthetic vision as well. In her architectural writings her sympathies lay more with the interior designer, who has to work within given constraints to make a house livable, than with the architect, who is given a much freer hand and, in her opinion, too often produces unlivable spaces in the name of artistic autonomy. This preference is in keeping with her vehement rejection of those artists and movements that, on the surface at least, most represented change, and that called for, among other things, the abandonment of the closed forms of mainstream genres. In her own fiction, Wharton frequently chose to work in the genre of the short story, which is the most constrained and concentrated of prose genres and which requires the writer to adapt to the predetermined literary conventions—such as a fast-paced beginning and a dramatic conclusion—that the public expected. At the same time, she recognized that stories must to some extent dictate their own structures. Since the competing claims of tradition and individual autonomy constitute a recurrent theme in Wharton's short stories, form reflects content in this case by incorporating the same dialectic of enclosure.
items.stemText: The passage suggests that Wharton would be most likely to agree with which one of the following statements about cultural traditions?

options.optionContent: Repressive traditions can be overcome only gradually because the beneficiaries of those traditions will work to preserve them.
The constraints imposed by tradition, burdensome though they may be, are valuable because of the culture they make possible.
Emphasizing the strengths of traditional culture rather than its weaknesses is essential in good literature.
People must resign themselves to the manner in which cultural traditions enclose them because traditions reflect a cultural consensus.
Traditions continue because they reflect an enduring wisdom greater than the ephemeral dictates of the individual will.

items.stemText: The passage suggests that the author would most likely agree with which one of the following statements about feminism during the period of Wharton's lifetime?

options.optionContent: Feminism mainly aimed to change middle-class women's lives.
Feminism inspired a large number of women to pursue successful careers as writers.
Feminism lost popularity because of its failure to make headway against the barriers to women's advancement.
Feminism could take different forms while still remaining committed to the advancement of women.
Feminism was based on the view that the key to women's advancement lay in eliminating the economic rather than the political obstacles confronting women.

items.stemText: The passage contains information that suggests which one of the following?

options.optionContent: In order to avoid alienating editors and readers, Wharton sacrificed her feminist ideals.
Wharton saw expanded political representation as the most effective way to improve women's condition.
Wharton likened society to an unlivable house that must be rebuilt from the ground up.
Wharton concentrated on the short story because it was the most lucrative of genres.
Wharton preferred piecemeal to wholesale experimentation in the arts.

items.stemText: Which one of the following best describes the author's attitude toward Wharton's working within the structure of the short story?

options.optionContent: indignation at the necessity for compromise
regret, tempered by admiration of Wharton's consequent economic independence
appreciation of the genre's suitability to Wharton's concerns
approval, because it opened the door for other women writers
ambivalence toward the external limitation imposed on Wharton's artistic expression

items.stemText: In the passage, the author primarily seeks to

options.optionContent: point out how Wharton created opportunities for self-expression within established conventions
explain how Wharton overcame society's constraints and became a financially successful novelist
contrast Wharton's life and attitudes with traditional stereotypes placed upon women
identify the origin of Wharton's interest in architecture and design
show that, contrary to appearances, Wharton was a feminist

items.stemText: The contrast between interior designer and architect in the third paragraph is presented as analogous to the contrast

options.optionContent: between artistic and social values in literature
between artists working within traditional genres and more experimental artists
between the short story and other, more open, literary forms
between the constraints that Wharton felt in her life and the freedom she experienced in her fiction
between feminists seeking financial self-sufficiency and those advocating more radical social change

items.stimulusText:
Marc Bloch's scholarship epitomizes the work of the Annales school, an approach to history named after the interdisciplinary French journal Annales, which Bloch founded in 1929 and for which Bloch was both a major contributor and an editor. Together with colleagues like Lucien Febvre, Bloch aspired to reform the way history was studied, taught, and written about. For the Annales scholars, history's central subject was not political events and leaders, but "l'homme": human beings whose world was to be rediscovered through research into the evidence concerning topics as diverse as fields, forests, huts, chapels, and counting rooms, as well as libraries, courtrooms, and palaces. The task of the historian, for these scholars, was not merely to publish facts that "speak for themselves," as positivists maintained. Instead, the historian was to take a more active interpretive role, arranging the material so as to integrate, for example, bygone economic practices with the prevailing social and political forms of their era. The historian was to probe the evidence to discover the assumptions and perceptions distinctive to an age. These made up its "mentalité."

Bloch's contributions to the Annales school are epitomized in several books that are still widely read and highly influential. The Royal Touch (1924) was a pacesetting exploration of mentalité, a study of the apparently miraculous power of English and French monarchs to cure the disease of scrofula by laying their hands on the afflicted. Bloch's work transformed old anecdotes about "the king's touch" into a history of the origins, meanings, and demise of ritual kingship. The Royal Touch has remained the freshest of all Bloch's books. French Rural History (1931) explored the basic patterns of French agriculture—with peasants in some regions farming across open fields, and in others farming within enclosures. Inspired in part by earlier German and English scholars, Bloch showed that present-day landscapes could teach us about the past, and he found fresh social meanings in old manorial charters by posing new questions they might answer. Finally, there was the two-volume Feudal Society, which appeared in 1939–1940. Here it was Bloch's aim to analyze the social structure characteristic of medieval times and to portray that which gave distinctness to those centuries.

He found the distinctive element in lordship, the subordinating through personal ties of loyalty of one person to another from the top of society to the bottom, from the monarch down through the ranks of vassals and vassals' own vassals, down to yeomen and serfs. Bloch showed how the centrality of the institution of lordship, and the reverence it was therefore accorded, shaped an era's thought, its prayer, even the conventions of love within the nobility into expressions of dependency.
items.stemText: Which one of the following titles best summarizes the content of the passage?

options.optionContent: "Marc Bloch's Methodology in the Historical Reconstruction of French Agriculture"
"The Concept of Mentalité in the Scholarship of the Annales School"
"Lordship as the Distinctive Element in Marc Bloch's Analysis of Feudal Society"
"Marc Bloch's Contributions to the Annales School"
"Ritual Kingship and Mentalité in the Writings of Marc Bloch"

items.stemText: According to the passage, Bloch's research on "the king's touch" is primarily concerned with the

options.optionContent: causes and cure of scrofula
origins of lordship
bond between monarchs and vassals
institution of ritual kingship
feudal nobility's forms of religious devotion

items.stemText: Bloch's scholarship is described in the passage as touching on all of the following except:

options.optionContent: social structure
agricultural patterns
political conflicts
healing powers
ties of loyalty

items.stemText: The author refers to l'homme (third sentence of the first paragraph) in order to

options.optionContent: express a similarity between institutions
characterize a school of historical scholarship
contrast two political systems
refer to an authoritative source
question an approach's validity

items.stemText: According to the passage, in their historical approach the Annales scholars

options.optionContent: emphasized the need to abstain from sweeping generalizations about past societies
stressed the primacy of economic forces in determining the nature of a culture
relied primarily on evidence from archival sources
emulated the successes of their positivist predecessors
explored aspects of a bygone culture that its contemporaries might have taken for granted

Marc Bloch's scholarship epitomizes the work of the Annales school, an approach to history named after the interdisciplinary French journal Annales, which Bloch founded in 1929 and for which Bloch was both a major contributor and an editor. Together with colleagues like Lucien Febvre, Bloch aspired to reform the way history was studied, taught, and written about. For the Annales scholars, history's central subject was not political events and leaders, but "l'homme": human beings whose world was to be rediscovered through research into the evidence concerning topics as diverse as fields, forests, huts, chapels, and counting rooms, as well as libraries, courtrooms, and palaces. The task of the historian, for these scholars, was not merely to publish facts that "speak for themselves," as positivists maintained. Instead, the historian was to take a more active interpretive role, arranging the material so as to integrate, for example, bygone economic practices with the prevailing social and political forms of their era. The historian was to probe the evidence to discover the assumptions and perceptions distinctive to an age. These made up its "mentalité."

Bloch's contributions to the Annales school are epitomized in several books that are still widely read and highly influential. The Royal Touch (1924) was a pacesetting exploration of mentalité, a study of the apparently miraculous power of English and French monarchs to cure the disease of scrofula by laying their hands on the afflicted. Bloch's work transformed old anecdotes about "the king's touch" into a history of the origins, meanings, and demise of ritual kingship. The Royal Touch has remained the freshest of all Bloch's books. French Rural History (1931) explored the basic patterns of French agriculture—with peasants in some regions farming across open fields, and in others farming within enclosures. Inspired in part by earlier German and English scholars, Bloch showed that present-day landscapes could teach us about the past, and he found fresh social meanings in old manorial charters by posing new questions they might answer. Finally, there was the two-volume Feudal Society, which appeared in 1939–1940. Here it was Bloch's aim to analyze the social structure characteristic of medieval times and to portray that which gave distinctness to those centuries.

He found the distinctive element in lordship, the subordinating through personal ties of loyalty of one person to another from the top of society to the bottom, from the monarch down through the ranks of vassals and vassals' own vassals, down to yeomen and serfs. Bloch showed how the centrality of the institution of lordship, and the reverence it was therefore accorded, shaped an era's thought, its prayer, even the conventions of love within the nobility into expressions of dependency.
items.stemText: Which one of the following statements would represent the most logical continuation of the passage?

options.optionContent: The work of many contemporary social historians owes much to Bloch and the Annales school.
The legacy of positivism went back at least to the naive attempt of nineteenth-century scholars to apply the methods of natural science to historical study.
The Royal Touch was important in shaping the way a generation of historians grasped the notion of mentalité, but the book has only antiquarian interest today.
The Annales scholars showed little interest in the mentalité or agricultural practices of medieval peasants.
Bloch's treatment of the intricate political maneuvers of Louis IX stands out as the crowning achievement of his scholarly career.

items.stemText: The author of the passage would be most likely to characterize Bloch's scholarly work as

options.optionContent: unique in its approach to material culture
innovative in its time and still of major importance
a refinement of the historical approach advocated by positivists
less relevant to the study of economic history than to the study of political history
less convincing in its interpretation of ritual kingship than in its account of ties of dependency

items.stemText: Which one of the following best illustrates the influence of what Bloch, according to the passage, regarded as the central institution of feudalism in the last sentence of the passage?

options.optionContent: peasant customs that required the setting aside of certain communally held lands for the grazing of livestock
ceremonies in which a vassal pledged military service to a monarch
medieval documents confirming that some vassals were in fact independent of local lords
reports that monarchs as late as the eighteenth century cured scrofula by touching the afflicted
ecclesiastical documents of the Middle Ages chronicling the history of Christianity

description: The Drill Set contains reading comprehension question types. Each question involves comprehending and analyzing lengthy and complex materials, encompassing both single and paired passages.
